title: Go Buffered Channels
content:
### Buffered Channels

Buffered channels have a capacity and only block when full.

### Creating Buffered Channels

```go
ch := make(chan int, 3) // buffer capacity of 3
```

### Behavior

```go
ch := make(chan int, 3)
ch <- 1  // no block
ch <- 2  // no block
ch <- 3  // no block
// ch <- 4 // BLOCKS: buffer is full

fmt.Println(<-ch) // 1 (buffer now has room)
ch <- 4           // OK
```

### Unbuffered vs Buffered

| Aspect | Unbuffered (cap=0) | Buffered (cap>0) |
|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| Send | Blocks until received | Blocks when full |
| Receive | Blocks until sent | Blocks when empty |
| Sync | Guaranteed sync | Async up to capacity |

### Len and Cap

```go
ch := make(chan int, 5)
ch <- 10
ch <- 20

fmt.Println(len(ch)) // 2 (elements in buffer)
fmt.Println(cap(ch)) // 5 (total capacity)
```

### Producer-Consumer Pattern

```go
func producer(ch chan<- int) {
    for i := 0; i < 10; i++ {
        ch <- i
        fmt.Println("Produced:", i)
    }
    close(ch)
}

func consumer(ch <-chan int) {
    for val := range ch {
        fmt.Println("Consumed:", val)
        time.Sleep(100 * time.Millisecond)
    }
}

func main() {
    ch := make(chan int, 5) // buffer smooths out production/consumption
    go producer(ch)
    consumer(ch)
}
```

### When to Use Buffered Channels

- Bursty workloads with occasional spikes
- Decoupling producer and consumer speeds
- Limiting parallelism (semaphore pattern)

> **Key Point:** A buffered channel with capacity 1 behaves like a mutex. A buffered channel of size N acts as a counting semaphore. Choose your buffer size carefully - too large wastes memory, too small causes blocking.
codeExample:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    ch := make(chan string, 2)

    ch <- "buffered"
    ch <- "channel"

    fmt.Println(<-ch)
    fmt.Println(<-ch)

    // Demonstrate len and cap
    ch2 := make(chan int, 5)
    fmt.Println("cap:", cap(ch2), "len:", len(ch2))
    ch2 <- 1
    ch2 <- 2
    fmt.Println("cap:", cap(ch2), "len:", len(ch2))
}

quiz.question: When does a send to a buffered channel block?
What does len(ch) return for a buffered channel?
